guest_role: AI experts: E-Mad (Stability), Des Travers (Intercom), Jan Lekoun (Meta), Richard Sotcher (You.com), Chris Ware (Runway), Tom Tungus (Theory Ventures), Miles Grimshaw (Benchmark), others
assertiveness_why: Harry is in curator/moderator role rather than interviewer; asks transitional topic questions but explicitly maintains neutrality between competing expert viewpoints; does not contest or plant theses; format fundamentally non-comparable to traditional single-guest interviews.
question_style: Curator/moderator questions introducing topics ('Will we see commoditization?', 'How important is model size?'); transitions between expert speakers; occasional synthesis ('That's interesting'); facilitates debate rather than drives inquiry.
tone: Warm but removed; host/curator role rather than peer interviewer. Treats speakers as equal experts. Ends by asking audience feedback on experimental format and whether to repeat. Neutral facilitator stance.
depth: Exceptional breadth across AI topics: LLM commoditization timelines, model size/parameters efficiency, open vs closed source dynamics, infrastructure (AWS, cloud) vs application layer value distribution, pricing model shifts (seat→consumption→outcome-based), co-pilot incumbent strategy vs agent disruption, incumbent positioning (Apple privacy, Google search threat, Amazon speed), AI's societal/jobs impact.
harry_quotes: Will we see the commoditization of LLMs?
How important is the size of these models?
Where does the value accrue—infrastructure or application layer?
What does the pricing model and business model look like for next-gen AI?
learnings: 5-6 foundational model companies will survive; LLMs commoditizing but narrowing gap is slow (close but not yet parity across providers).

Model size decreasing in importance; smaller models fine-tuned with proprietary data outperform; can run on-device/laptop (Jan Lekoun's position vs Richard Sotcher's counterpoint on parameter importance).
Open source eventually competes with proprietary; universities and researchers creating open alternatives; Meta leading open-source push; economics of scale currently favor OpenAI but window closing.
Infrastructure layer (3 winners: AWS, GCP, Azure) and application layer (100+ companies) capture equivalent market cap; odds of success higher in applications due to diversity of needs.
Pricing shifts from seat-based → consumption-based → outcome-based (SLA on work delivered, not software uptime); work shifts from users doing data entry to AI agents doing work.
Co-pilots are incumbent defensibility strategy; disruptive opportunity is agent/pilot that replaces application rather than augments it.

Apple best positioned for on-device LLM privacy; Siri becomes useful; Google existentially threatened by search disruption (Alt: sponsored injection ads into LLM answers); Amazon moving fast via Bedrock.

AI job displacement is not technological inevitability but political/social distribution problem; every tech revolution creates more jobs but profits concentrate without policy intervention (income tax, social programs).
notable: Thanksgiving special experimental format: curated audio clips from multiple prior 20VC episodes edited into thematic panel debate. Harry is curator/moderator, not traditional interviewer. Explicitly asks audience feedback on format repeatability. No traditional sponsor reads. Harry's role is fundamentally different—editorial curation vs direct engagement—making assertiveness rubric non-comparable to single-guest episodes.
